question: Will there be more 'Protests' in Nigeria for the 30 days before 2026-06-28 compared to the 30-day average of 'Protests' over the 360 days preceding 2026-06-21? e.g. If the forecast due date is 2024-01-01 and we have the following data: Date,'Protests' 2023-11-11,1 2023-10-10,2 to calculate the 30-day average of 'Protests' over the preceding 360 days, we’d have: (1+2)/12=0.25. In this example, for the question to resolve positively, 1 or more 'Protests' would need to occur in the 30 days leading up to the resolution. Resolution criteria: Resolves to the value calculated from the ACLED dataset once the data is published. Background: ACLED classifies events into six distinct categories: 1. Battles: violent interactions between two organized armed groups at a particular time and location; 2. Protests: in-person public demonstrations of three or more participants in which the participants do not engage in violence, though violence may be used against them; 3. Riots: violent events where demonstrators or mobs of three or more engage in violent or destructive acts, including but not limited to physical fights, rock throwing, property destruction, etc.; 4. Explosions/Remote violence: incidents in which one side uses weapon types that, by their nature, are at range and widely destructive; 5. Violence against civilians: violent events where an organized armed group inflicts violence upon unarmed non-combatants; and 6. Strategic developments: contextually important information regarding incidents and activities of groups that are not recorded as any of the other event types, yet may trigger future events or contribute to political dynamics within and across states. Detailed information about the categories can be found at: https://acleddata.com/knowledge-base/codebook/#acled-events

Date,'Protests'
2023-11-11,1
2023-10-10,2
to calculate the 30-day average of 'Protests' over the preceding 360 days, we’d have: (1+2)/12=0.25.

In this example, for the question to resolve positively, 1 or more 'Protests' would need to occur in the 30 days leading up to the resolution.

Resolution criteria: Resolves to the value calculated from the ACLED dataset once the data is published.

Background: ACLED classifies events into six distinct categories:

1. Battles: violent interactions between two organized armed groups at a particular time and
   location;
2. Protests: in-person public demonstrations of three or more participants in which the participants
   do not engage in violence, though violence may be used against them;
3. Riots: violent events where demonstrators or mobs of three or more engage in violent or
   destructive acts, including but not limited to physical fights, rock throwing, property
   destruction, etc.;
4. Explosions/Remote violence: incidents in which one side uses weapon types that, by their nature,
   are at range and widely destructive;
5. Violence against civilians: violent events where an organized armed group inflicts violence upon
   unarmed non-combatants; and
6. Strategic developments: contextually important information regarding incidents and activities of
   groups that are not recorded as any of the other event types, yet may trigger future events or
   contribute to political dynamics within and across states.

Detailed information about the categories can be found at:
https://acleddata.com/knowledge-base/codebook/#acled-events

By and large these peaceful demonstrations were centred around major urban areas and political hubs such as the Federal Capital District (Abuja), Lagos, and Port Harcourt. These areas historically have less civil instability, greater democratic participation of citizens, and more comprehensive government control as opposed to more rural areas where insurgency and agricultural conflicts breed unstable climates. Civilians in these latter areas are less protected by government legislation and more susceptible to violence from local militias, rebel forces, and vigilante groups. evidences.content: There is little sense of national cohesion in Nigeria, and protests tend to be along ethnic and religious lines rather than national. Like the 2012 demonstrations against the Goodluck Jonathan administration’s efforts to reduce the fuel subsidy, current protests against the police are exceptional in that they appear to be occurring across the country, bridging the usual ethnic and religious boundaries. They also appear to be strongly supported by the Nigerian diaspora, which is linking them to Black Lives Matter demonstrations in the United States and Europe. The 2012 demonstrations collapsed in the aftermath of minor government concessions and the buying-off of certain trade unions. The coming weeks will show whether these demonstrations have any greater staying power. evidences.content: Demonstrations against the removal of the fuel subsidy began on 2 January in Lagos, Abuja, and Kano. Protesters gathered in central areas of the cities to demand that the government reinstate the subsidy. By 6 January, protests had taken place in every major Nigerian city, with nearly all economic activity stopped in Lagos, Ibadan, and Kano. Protesters demonstrated in central areas of the cities, and they focused on their support for the fuel subsidy and anger with government corruption. Although protesters consisted somewhat disproportionately of the poor, they came from a wide range of backgrounds. Protests were especially well-attended in the northern city of Kano. evidences.title: ACLED Codebook
evidences.content: Dates are a necessary component of all ACLED events. ACLED events are atomic as events are recorded by day. If a military campaign in an area starts on 1 March 2020 and lasts until 5 March 2020 with violent activity reported on each day, this is recorded as five different events, with a different date for each entry. This episode would not be entered as a single campaign of violence. This allows ACLED to record the exact number of active days of a conflict. Events that the source material indicates as occurring in the space of three months — such as long-running protests — are not automatically assumed to have 90 days of activity, but instead are only recorded for the days for which it is specifically reported that an activity took place. This avoids over-counting event occurrences. [...] As ACLED records a wide range of constituent events for periods and areas affected by political violence and disorder, more information is collected than datasets that focus on specific forms of violence (e.g., civil war, terrorism as defined by targeting of civilians by non-state actors alone, or those with fatality thresholds for inclusion) or campaigns of violence rather than events. evidences.content: In many countries, including Nigeria, fuel subsidies are regarded as part of an implicit social contract between citizens and the state (Beblawi and Luciani, 1987; Hertog, 2017; Luciani, 1990; Fattouh et al., 2016; Moerenhout et al., 2017). Therefore, reducing subsidies without improving social welfare protection may be considered a unilateral change in the social contract, which can give rise to protest and political instability (Moerenhout, 2018a, b; Luciani, 1990). Conversely, if people believe that the state is fulfilling its obligations by providing better services and transparently investing the savings from fuel subsidy reform into sectors of direct relevance to households (e.g., health, education, and infrastructure), then they may be willing to accept subsidy reform, in the same [...] This paper provides rigorous empirical evidence about the determinants of support for fuel subsidy reforms in Africa's largest economy – Nigeria. In reality, Nigeria has operated a multi-layered subsidy regime across three critical domains: fuel pricing, foreign exchange management, and electricity tariffs. Each of these represents a variant of the same fiscal phenomenon—the underpricing of scarce economic resources, with the resulting cost absorbed by the state, whether explicitly, implicitly, or opaquely. [...] Is this approach primarily driven by constitutional and institutional constraints within Nigeria’s fiscal federalism framework, or are there strategic considerations (such as the need to incentivize subnational economic activity and maintain stability) that justify it? Furthermore, to what extent do political economy factors shape these decisions, and how sustainable is this model over the medium to long term? 9w 2 [...] Every serious reform compresses before it expands.
